generation_settings.grammar:
boolean ::= ("true" | "false") space
char ::= [^"\\\x7F\x00-\x1F] | [\\] (["\\bfnrt] | "u" [0-9a-fA-F]{4})
decimal-part ::= [0-9]{1,16}
integral-part ::= [0] | [1-9] [0-9]{0,15}
json-array ::= "[" space ("]" | json-value ("," space json-value)* space "]") space
json-bool ::= ("true" | "false") space
json-null ::= "null" space
json-number ::= "-"? ("0" | [1-9] [0-9]*) ("." [0-9]+)? (("e" | "E") [+-]? [0-9]+)?  space
json-object ::= "{" space ("}" | json-string space ":" space json-value (space "," space json-string space ":" space json-value)* space "}") space
json-string ::= "\"" ( [^"\\] | "\\" ( ["\\/ bfnrt] | "u" [0-9a-fA-F]{4} ) )* "\"" space
json-value ::= json-object | json-array | json-string | json-number | json-bool | json-null
number ::= ("-"? integral-part) ("." decimal-part)? ([eE] [-+]? integral-part)? space
root ::= tool-call
space ::= | " " | "\n"{1,2} [ \t]{0,20}
string ::= "\"" char* "\"" space
tool-apply-patch ::= (" to=functions." "apply_patch" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-apply-patch-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "apply_patch" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-apply-patch-schema
tool-apply-patch-schema ::= "{" space tool-apply-patch-schema-patch-kv "}" space
tool-apply-patch-schema-patch-kv ::= "\"patch\"" space ":" space string
tool-call ::= tool-run-process | tool-fetch | tool-apply-patch | tool-semantic-grep
tool-fetch ::= (" to=functions." "fetch" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-fetch-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "fetch" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-fetch-schema
tool-fetch-schema ::= "{" space tool-fetch-schema-url-kv ( "," space ( tool-fetch-schema-start-index-kv tool-fetch-schema-start-index-rest | tool-fetch-schema-max-length-kv tool-fetch-schema-max-length-rest | tool-fetch-schema-raw-kv ) )? "}" space
tool-fetch-schema-max-length ::= ([1-9] | ([1-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9]) | ([1-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9])) | ([1-8] [0-9]{3} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9]))) | ([1-8] [0-9]{4} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{3} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9])))) | ([1-8] [0-9]{5} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{4} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{3} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9]{2} | [9] ([0-8] [0-9] | [9] [0-9])))))) space
tool-fetch-schema-max-length-kv ::= "\"max_length\"" space ":" space tool-fetch-schema-max-length
tool-fetch-schema-max-length-rest ::= ( "," space tool-fetch-schema-raw-kv )?
tool-fetch-schema-raw-kv ::= "\"raw\"" space ":" space boolean
tool-fetch-schema-start-index ::= ([0] | [1-9] [0-9]{0,15}) space
tool-fetch-schema-start-index-kv ::= "\"start_index\"" space ":" space tool-fetch-schema-start-index
tool-fetch-schema-start-index-rest ::= ( "," space tool-fetch-schema-max-length-kv )? tool-fetch-schema-max-length-rest
tool-fetch-schema-url ::= "\"" char+ "\"" space
tool-fetch-schema-url-kv ::= "\"url\"" space ":" space tool-fetch-schema-url
tool-run-process ::= (" to=functions." "run_process" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-run-process-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "run_process" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-run-process-schema
tool-run-process-schema ::= "{" space  (tool-run-process-schema-command-line-kv tool-run-process-schema-command-line-rest | tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-kv tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-rest | tool-run-process-schema-cwd-kv tool-run-process-schema-cwd-rest | tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-kv tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-rest | tool-run-process-schema-argv-kv )? "}" space
tool-run-process-schema-argv ::= "[" space string ("," space string)* "]" space
tool-run-process-schema-argv-kv ::= "\"argv\"" space ":" space tool-run-process-schema-argv
tool-run-process-schema-command-line-kv ::= "\"command_line\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process-schema-command-line-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-kv )? tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-rest
tool-run-process-schema-cwd-kv ::= "\"cwd\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process-schema-cwd-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-kv )? tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-rest
tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-kv ::= "\"stdin_text\"" space ":" space string
tool-run-process-schema-stdin-text-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-cwd-kv )? tool-run-process-schema-cwd-rest
tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-kv ::= "\"timeout_ms\"" space ":" space number
tool-run-process-schema-timeout-ms-rest ::= ( "," space tool-run-process-schema-argv-kv )?
tool-semantic-grep ::= (" to=functions." "semantic_grep" "<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-semantic-grep-schema | ("<|channel|>" ("commentary" | "analysis") " to=functions." "semantic_grep" (space "<|constrain|>"? [A-Za-z0-9_-]+)? "<|message|>") tool-semantic-grep-schema
tool-semantic-grep-schema ::= "{" space tool-semantic-grep-schema-query-kv ( "," space ( tool-semantic-grep-schema-embed-top-k-kv tool-semantic-grep-schema-embed-top-k-rest | tool-semantic-grep-schema-instruct-kv tool-semantic-grep-schema-instruct-rest | tool-semantic-grep-schema-top-k-kv tool-semantic-grep-schema-top-k-rest | tool-semantic-grep-schema-filetype-kv ) )? "}" space
tool-semantic-grep-schema-embed-top-k-kv ::= "\"embed_top_k\"" space ":" space number
tool-semantic-grep-schema-embed-top-k-rest ::= ( "," space tool-semantic-grep-schema-instruct-kv )? tool-semantic-grep-schema-instruct-rest
tool-semantic-grep-schema-filetype-kv ::= "\"filetype\"" space ":" space string
tool-semantic-grep-schema-instruct-kv ::= "\"instruct\"" space ":" space string
tool-semantic-grep-schema-instruct-rest ::= ( "," space tool-semantic-grep-schema-top-k-kv )? tool-semantic-grep-schema-top-k-rest
tool-semantic-grep-schema-query-kv ::= "\"query\"" space ":" space string
tool-semantic-grep-schema-top-k-kv ::= "\"top_k\"" space ":" space number
tool-semantic-grep-schema-top-k-rest ::= ( "," space tool-semantic-grep-schema-filetype-kv )?

__verbose.prompt:
<|start|>system<|message|>You are ChatGPT, a large language model trained by OpenAI. Knowledge cutoff: 2024-06 Current date: 2026-04-06 Reasoning: medium # Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message. Calls to these tools must go to the commentary channel: 'functions'.<|end|><|start|>developer<|message|># Instructions You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo. ## Ground rules when writing code: - Follow the user's instructions carefully. - Do not explain answers using code comments. - NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.** - Never add new comments to the end of a line. - Never add trivial comments. - Respect indentation when modifying code. - Prefer readable code over of comments. - Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names. ## Tool use For tool use, never modify files outside of the current working directory unless the user requested it: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim ## Use `semantic_grep` to find code The semantic_grep tool: - has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase - enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval) - includes re-ranker - it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it! - while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg` ## Committing - `git diff` is a sanity check! - COMMIT when you're done. - Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b" ## 🙌 TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS - 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents - 🙌 `fd` to search file names ## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom. - ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R` - ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R` - ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f` Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions. ## Use `apply_patch` to edit files. Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope: *** Begin Patch [ one or more file sections ] *** End Patch Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations. You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking. Each operation starts with one of three headers: *** Add File: - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents). *** Delete File: - remove an existing file. Nothing follows. *** Update File: - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename). If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with: *** Move to: Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header). Within a hunk each line starts with: - for inserted text, * for removed text, or space ( ) for context. At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File. Here is the grammar: Patch := Begin { FileOp } End Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE } DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk } MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ] HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE A full patch can combine several operations: *** Begin Patch *** Add File: hello.txt +Hello world *** Update File: src/app.py *** Move to: src/main.py @@ def greet(): -print("Hi") +print("Hello, world!") *** Delete File: obsolete.txt *** End Patch It is important to remember: - You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update) - You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file Tips on a failure: - You only need enough context for a unique match! - The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed. - Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately! - Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines Please be curteous: - NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo. - Check your work! Especially after a failure. ### More Examples Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type: ```diff *** Begin Patch *** Update File: src/gap.py @@ @dataclass -class GapContext: - win: GapWindow - gap_rms: TimeSeries - analysis: AudioAnalysisResult +class GapContext: + win: SilenceWindow + gap_rms: TimeSeries + analysis: AudioAnalysisResult *** End Patch ``` Which can be simplified to: ```diff *** Begin Patch *** Update File: src/gap.py @@ -class GapContext: - win: GapWindow +class GapContext: + win: SilenceWindow *** End Patch ``` Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique: ```diff *** Begin Patch *** Update File: src/gap.py @@ - win: GapWindow + win: SilenceWindow *** End Patch ``` # Tools ## functions namespace functions { // Run a process on this darwin machine type run_process = (_: { // Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'. command_line?: string, // Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents. stdin_text?: string, // Optional to set working directory cwd?: string, // Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms timeout_ms?: number, // Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'. argv?: string[], }) => any; // Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown. Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that. type fetch = (_: { // On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required. start_index?: number, // default: 0, // URL to fetch url: string, // Maximum number of characters to return. max_length?: number, // default: 5000, // Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification. raw?: boolean, // default: false, }) => any; // Patch a file type apply_patch = (_: { // file changes in custom diff format patch: string, }) => any; // Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results. type semantic_grep = (_: { // number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking embed_top_k?: number, // instructions for the query, explain the type of query instruct?: string, // number of matches to return (post reranking) top_k?: number, // query text, what you are looking for query: string, // limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes filetype?: string, }) => any; } // namespace functions<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## General Code Preferences - When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is. - When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example: ```lua local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$") if is_blank_line then -- ... end ``` <|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## Lua Code Preferences Naming - Use snake_case for functions and variables. - Use PascalCase for classes. Type Hints - Prefer EmmyLua/LuaLS style annotations - Always annotate when type inference would be ambiguous, or for public APIs. Examples: ```lua ---@param names string[] ---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean ---@return table function process_names(names, checker) end ---@class WindowController ---@field window hs.axuielement ---@field title? string ---@field size { w: number, h: number } local WindowController = {} ``` <|end|><|start|>user<|message|># Semantic Grep matches: 5 This is automatic context based on my request. These may or may not be relevant. ## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/tools/router.lua:8-36 function M.openai_tools() local tools = {} -- * inject system message instructions based on available tools local system_instructs = {} for name, mcp_tool in pairs(mcp.tools_available) do table.insert(tools, openai_tool(mcp_tool)) local tool_instructs = mcp.get_system_message_instructions(name) if tool_instructs then table.insert(system_instructs, tool_instructs) end end for _, inprocess_tool in pairs(inprocess.tools_available) do -- PRN push this into inprocess module like mcp/init.lua above? if inprocess_tool["function"].name == "semantic_grep" then -- somewhat strange but neovim invocations are rooted in the current buffer, so I have to use that to dictate some tool availability even though agent like requests are buffer independent... since my LS client is tied to a buffer in neovim, gotta roll with it! NBD TBH if client.is_lsp_client_available() then table.insert(tools, inprocess_tool) end else table.insert(tools, inprocess_tool) if inprocess_tool["function"].name == "apply_patch" then table.insert(system_instructs, apply_patch_tool.get_system_message_instructions()) end end end return tools, system_instructs end ## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/tools/router.lua:0-19 local mcp = require("ask-openai.tools.mcp") local inprocess = require("ask-openai.tools.inprocess") local plumbing = require("ask-openai.tools.plumbing") local apply_patch_tool = require("ask-openai.tools.inproc.apply_patch") local client = require("ask-openai.rag.client.client") local M = {} function M.openai_tools() local tools = {} -- * inject system message instructions based on available tools local system_instructs = {} for name, mcp_tool in pairs(mcp.tools_available) do table.insert(tools, openai_tool(mcp_tool)) local tool_instructs = mcp.get_system_message_instructions(name) if tool_instructs then table.insert(system_instructs, tool_instructs) end end ## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/init.lua:127-137 function M.ask_openai() local cmdline = vim.fn.getcmdline() print("asking...") -- overwrites showing luaeval("...") in cmdline local stdin_text = ' env: nvim (neovim) command mode (return a valid command w/o the leading : ) \n question: ' .. cmdline local suggest = require("ask-openai.suggest") local result = suggest.get_vim_command_suggestion(stdin_text) return trim_null_characters(result) end ## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/config/init.lua:0-19 local local_share = require("ask-openai.config.local_share") ---@class Provider ---@field get_bearer_token fun(): string ---@field check fun() # optional ---@field get_chat_completions_url fun(): string # optional local M = {} --- ask-openai options ---@class AskOpenAIOptions ---@field model string ---@field provider string ---@field copilot CopilotOptions ---@field api_url string|nil local default_options = { keymaps = { cmdline_ask = "", }, ## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/tools/inprocess.lua:0-12 local apply_patch_tool = require("ask-openai.tools.inproc.apply_patch") local plumbing = require("ask-openai.tools.plumbing") local semantic_grep_tool = require("ask-openai.tools.inproc.semantic_grep") local M = {} ---@type OpenAITool[] M.tools_available = { semantic_grep = semantic_grep_tool.ToolDefinition, apply_patch = apply_patch_tool.ToolDefinition, } <|end|><|start|>user<|message|>/tools can you test accessing google.com<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>commentary to=functions.fetch <|constrain|>json<|message|>{"url": "https://www.google.com", "max_length": 500}<|call|><|start|>functions.fetch to=assistant<|channel|>commentary<|message|>{"isError":false,"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Contents of https://www.google.com/:\n

Google

GmailImages\n\nSign in\n\nAdvertisingBusiness SolutionsAbout Google\n\n© 2026 - Privacy - Terms\n\nGoogle apps"}]}<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>Here’s a quick check that the URL can be reached: ``` Contents of https://www.google.com/:

Google

GmailImages Sign in AdvertisingBusiness SolutionsAbout Google © 2026 - Privacy - Terms Google apps ``` The request succeeded and returned HTML content from Google’s homepage.<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>cool how about ansible-inventory docs <|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>We need to fetch Ansible inventory docs. Let's fetch that.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>commentary to=functions.fetch <|constrain|>json<|message|>{"url": "https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/inventory_guide/intro_inventory.html", "max_length": 1000}<|call|><|start|>functions.fetch to=assistant<|channel|>commentary<|message|>{"isError":false,"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Contents of https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/inventory_guide/intro_inventory.html:\n

messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

For tool use, never modify files outside of the current working directory unless the user requested it:
~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b"


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope:

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch

Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.
You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.
Each operation starts with one of three headers:

*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).

If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with:
*** Move to: <new path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).
Within a hunk each line starts with:

- for inserted text,
* for removed text, or
  space ( ) for context.
  At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File.

Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

It is important to remember:

- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)
- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Lua Code Preferences

Naming
- Use snake_case for functions and variables.
- Use PascalCase for classes.

Type Hints
- Prefer EmmyLua/LuaLS style annotations
- Always annotate when type inference would be ambiguous, or for public APIs.

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/tools/router.lua:8-36
function M.openai_tools()
    local tools = {}

    -- * inject system message instructions based on available tools
    local system_instructs = {}
    for name, mcp_tool in pairs(mcp.tools_available) do
        table.insert(tools, openai_tool(mcp_tool))
        local tool_instructs = mcp.get_system_message_instructions(name)
        if tool_instructs then
            table.insert(system_instructs, tool_instructs)
        end
    end
    for _, inprocess_tool in pairs(inprocess.tools_available) do
        -- PRN push this into inprocess module like mcp/init.lua above?

        if inprocess_tool["function"].name == "semantic_grep" then
            -- somewhat strange but neovim invocations are rooted in the current buffer, so I have to use that to dictate some tool availability even though agent like requests are buffer independent... since my LS client is tied to a buffer in neovim, gotta roll with it! NBD TBH
            if client.is_lsp_client_available() then
                table.insert(tools, inprocess_tool)
            end
        else
            table.insert(tools, inprocess_tool)
            if inprocess_tool["function"].name == "apply_patch" then
                table.insert(system_instructs, apply_patch_tool.get_system_message_instructions())
            end
        end
    end
    return tools, system_instructs
end

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/init.lua:127-137
function M.ask_openai()
    local cmdline = vim.fn.getcmdline()
    print("asking...") -- overwrites showing luaeval("...") in cmdline

    local stdin_text = ' env: nvim (neovim) command mode (return a valid command w/o the leading : ) \n question: ' ..
        cmdline

    local suggest = require("ask-openai.suggest")
    local result = suggest.get_vim_command_suggestion(stdin_text)
    return trim_null_characters(result)
end

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

function.description: Run a process on this darwin machine
function.name: run_process

start_index.title: Start Index
start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

raw.title: Raw
raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.

function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.
function.name: fetch

patch.description: file changes in custom diff format

embed_top_k.description: number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking

instruct.description: instructions for the query, explain the type of query

top_k.description: number of matches to return (post reranking)

query.description: query text, what you are looking for

filetype.description: limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes

function.name: semantic_grep
function.description: Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
